title: Wrought Processes and Discontinuities
narrationScript: Take a cast ingot and squeeze it. Hammer it, roll it, push it through a die. That is wrought product in one sentence, and the squeezing changes everything. Hot working breaks up the coarse cast grain structure, closes some of the internal voids, and strings the surviving discontinuities out along the direction of work. A wrought part is stronger and more uniform than the casting it came from, but it is not perfect. It carries two families of flaws. The first family is inherited. The pipe, porosity, inclusions, and segregation that came with the ingot are still there, now flattened and elongated. The second family is new, created by the working process itself. And here is the most important sentence in this lesson for a radiographer. Wrought discontinuities are mostly thin, tight, and planar, and they lie parallel to the direction of working, which usually means parallel to the surface. Radiography only sees a discontinuity when it changes the thickness of metal along the beam. A paper thin lamination lying flat in a plate changes almost nothing along a beam shot square through that plate, so the film comes out clean over a flaw that ultrasonics would find instantly. Know what the process makes, and know whether your method can see it. Start with forging. Forging works hot metal between dies, open dies for big shafts, discs, and rings, closed impression dies for shaped parts, with the excess squeezed out as flash and trimmed away. Done right, the grain flow follows the part contour and the forging is the strongest product form there is. Done wrong, it makes laps and bursts. A lap is surface metal that folded over itself, picked up oxide, and pressed flat without fusing. It is tight, it lies at a shallow angle to the surface, and it is a poor target for radiography. Magnetic particle finds laps. A burst is an internal rupture from forging the metal too cold or taking too big a reduction in one blow. Bursts live in the center of the section, and ultrasonics owns them. Very heavy steel forgings can also suffer hydrogen flakes, fine internal fissures from dissolved hydrogen during cooling, again ultrasonic territory. Add the inherited defects, leftover pipe, segregation bands, and inclusion stringers, and you have the forging flaw list. Now rolling. A slab goes through stand after stand and comes out as plate, sheet, or strip. A bloom or billet becomes bars and structural shapes. Whatever was inside gets flattened too. Trapped pipe and large inclusions become laminations, wide thin separations lying dead parallel to the plate surface, often at mid thickness. Inclusions in bars stretch into stringers, fine lines running with the bar axis. Surface defects on the billet, cracks, tears, and rolled over blowholes, are drawn out into seams, tight longitudinal surface discontinuities that may run the full length of a bar. Laminations are found with straight beam ultrasonics, or where they break out at cut edges and weld preps. Seams are magnetic particle and eddy current work. Radiography sees stringers and open centerline defects only when the beam lines up along the plane of the flaw, and it sees flat laminations essentially not at all. Extrusion pushes a heated billet through a shaped die, like toothpaste from a tube, and the product carries the die profile. Tubes, channels, and complex aluminum shapes, and with molten glass lubrication even steel sections. Its signature internal defect is the chevron, also called a center burst, a chain of arrowhead shaped ruptures spaced down the centerline. They form when the reduction is too light or the die angle too steep, so the deformation never reaches the center of the billet and the centerline is dragged forward in tension until it tears. Shot broadside, chevrons image as a line of dark V shaped indications, one of the few wrought discontinuities radiography finds well. The other one to know is the back end defect, sometimes called extrusion pipe or coring. Near the end of the stroke, the oxidized skin of the billet flows inward and rides into the product as a ring of oxide buried near the back end. Mills crop and discard the back end for exactly this reason, and a radiographer who sees a ring shaped dark indication near the trailing end of an extrusion should think of it immediately. Too much speed or heat also tears the surface, the so called fir tree cracking. So when a wrought part lands in your bay, ask three questions. What did this start as. What did the process do to the flaws it inherited. And does the geometry let my beam line up with the planes those flaws lie on. If the answer to the last question is no, the honest call is a different method, not a clean report.
imagePrompt: A red-hot steel slab emerges between the massive rolls of a hot rolling mill, scale flying and steam rising off the descaled surface, while an operator watches from the control pulpit window.

sections.heading: From Cast to Wrought
sections.body:
**Wrought** products are made by mechanically working cast stock - hammering, pressing, rolling, extruding - usually hot. Working refines the coarse as-cast grain, closes some internal voids, and dramatically improves strength and toughness. It also does two things the radiographer must internalize. First, it **inherits** the ingot's discontinuities: pipe, blowholes, inclusions, and segregation are not destroyed, only reshaped. Second, it **orients** them: deformation strings every survivor out along the direction of working, so wrought discontinuities are characteristically thin, tight, and **planar**, lying parallel to the worked surface or the product axis.

That orientation is the heart of the inspection problem. Radiography registers a discontinuity only as a change in the metal path the beam traverses. A volumetric pore is missing metal in every direction and images from any angle. A tight planar separation lying perpendicular to the beam presents almost no missing path - the film stays clean. The same separation aligned **with** the beam presents its full depth and prints as a sharp dark line. Castings, full of volumetric flaws, are radiography's natural territory; wrought products, full of tight planar flaws parallel to the surface, frequently are not, and ultrasonic, magnetic particle, or eddy current methods take over. A Level II who understands this never offers a clean film as proof of a clean plate.

sections.heading: Forgings
sections.body:
Forging shapes hot metal by compressive blows or steady squeeze - **open-die** forging between flat or simple tools for shafts, discs, and rings; **closed-die** (impression) forging in shaped cavities, with excess metal escaping as **flash** that is trimmed off. Proper forging yields the strongest product form there is, with grain flow following the part contour. Its characteristic discontinuities:

- **Laps** - surface metal folded over itself and pressed flat without fusing, caused by poor die radii, oversized blanks, or mislocated stock. A lap is tight, oxide-lined, and lies at a shallow angle to the surface - a magnetic particle target and a poor radiographic one.
- **Bursts** - internal ruptures torn open when the metal was worked too cold or reduced too much in one bite; they concentrate at the center of the section. Ultrasonics owns bursts; radiography may show one only if it is open and favorably oriented.
- **Flash-line cracks** from trimming, and surface cracking from improper working temperature.
- **Hydrogen flakes** - fine internal fissures in heavy steel forgings from hydrogen dissolved during melting and released during cooling; found ultrasonically.
- **Inherited defects** - residual pipe, segregation bands, and inclusion stringers elongated along the grain flow.

When forgings are radiographed at all, it is usually for specific geometries or weld repairs; the routine volumetric method for forgings is ultrasonic testing.

sections.caption: Where the wrought processes create their characteristic discontinuities: laps and bursts in forging, laminations and seams in rolling, chevrons and the back-end defect in extrusion.

sections.heading: Rolled Products
sections.body:
Rolling squeezes a slab, bloom, or billet between powered rolls, pass after pass, into plate, sheet, strip, bar, rod, and structural shapes. Reduction flattens and lengthens everything inside the steel:

- **Laminations** - residual pipe, large inclusions, and porosity flattened into wide, paper-thin separations lying **parallel to the plate surface**, often at mid-thickness. Plate for pressure parts is scanned ultrasonically precisely because a straight-through radiograph cannot see a lamination: the beam crosses essentially the same metal path with or without it. Laminations betray themselves at cut edges and weld preps - where magnetic particle finds them - and they matter most when a joint loads the plate through its thickness.
- **Stringers** - nonmetallic inclusions drawn into long fine lines running with the rolling direction in bars and billets. With the beam broadside to a bar, stringers can image as faint dark streaks along the axis.
- **Seams** - billet surface cracks, tears, and rolled-over blowholes drawn into tight, oxide-lined longitudinal surface discontinuities that may run the length of a bar. Magnetic particle and eddy current are the production methods for seams.
- **Slivers and rolled-in scale** - loose surface flakes and oxide pressed into the surface during rolling.

The pattern repeats: rolled-product discontinuities are tight, planar, and surface-parallel - the orientation radiography sees worst.

sections.title: Orientation decides detectability
sections.body: Radiography sees a discontinuity only as a **change in metal path along the beam**. Volumetric flaws (pores, slag, shrink) image from any angle. Tight planar flaws image only when the beam runs **along their plane** - and wrought processing lays them parallel to the surface, the worst case for a straight-through shot. A clean radiograph of plate is **not** evidence the plate is free of laminations; that is ultrasonic territory, and the honest report says so.

sections.caption: The same plate, two flaw orientations: a lamination parallel to the surface leaves the film blank, while a planar separation aligned with the beam prints a sharp dark line.

sections.heading: Extruded Products
sections.body:
Extrusion rams a heated billet through a shaped die opening, producing tube and constant-cross-section profiles - the dominant process for aluminum shapes, and, with molten-glass lubrication, hot-extruded steel as well. Three discontinuities define it:

- **Chevron cracking (center bursts)** - a chain of arrowhead-shaped internal ruptures spaced along the centerline. When the reduction is too light or the die angle too steep, deformation never penetrates to the center; the centerline is dragged forward in tension and tears periodically. Shot broadside, chevrons print as a line of dark V-shaped indications - one of the few wrought discontinuities radiography finds well.
- **Back-end defect** (extrusion pipe, coring) - toward the end of the stroke the oxidized billet skin and surface dross flow inward and ride into the product as an annular, oxide-lined discontinuity buried near the back end. Mills crop the back end generously for this reason; a ring-like dark indication near the trailing end of an extrusion should raise it immediately.
- **Speed (fir-tree) cracking** - transverse surface tearing when temperature and ram speed make the surface hot-short.

Billet surface defects also draw out into longitudinal die lines and seams, and inherited inclusions become stringers, exactly as in rolling.

sections.caption: Wrought discontinuities: origin, orientation, and the method that finds them.
rows: Surface metal folded over without fusion
Poor RT target (tight, shallow angle) - MT/PT
Internal rupture - worked too cold or too fast
UT primary; RT only if open and aligned
Pipe and inclusions flattened parallel to the surface
Invisible to straight-through RT - UT scan
Inclusions elongated in the rolling direction
Faint axial streaks possible on RT; UT/MT
Billet surface defects drawn out, oxide-lined
Centerline tension - light reduction, steep die angle
Good RT target broadside to the axis
Oxidized skin flows into the center at end of stroke
Annular ring near the back end on RT; cropped off

figures.title: Orientation decides what the film shows

checkQuestions.question: A plate containing a large mid-thickness lamination is radiographed with the beam normal to the surface. Why does the film show nothing?
options: The lamination is too deep for the radiation to reach
Laminations scatter radiation evenly in all directions
The tight, surface-parallel separation barely changes the metal path along the beam, so there is no density difference to record
Film cannot resolve discontinuities longer than one inch
checkQuestions.explanation: RT records changes in transmitted intensity, which require a change in metal path along the beam; a paper-thin separation lying perpendicular to the beam removes almost no path, so plate is laminated-checked with ultrasonics instead.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best describes a forging lap?
options: A volumetric internal void at the center of the section
Surface metal folded over and pressed flat without fusing, oxide-lined and lying at a shallow angle to the surface
A chemical segregation band from the original ingot
A crack caused by quenching after heat treatment
checkQuestions.explanation: Laps form when poor die radii, oversized blanks, or mislocated stock fold surface metal over itself; the tight, shallow-angle, oxide-lined fold is a classic magnetic particle find and a poor radiographic target.

checkQuestions.question: Chevron (center burst) cracking in an extrusion is caused by:
options: Excessive lubrication of the die
Moisture in the billet surface
Centrifugal force during the push
Light reduction or too steep a die angle leaving the centerline in tension, so it tears periodically as it is dragged forward
checkQuestions.explanation: When deformation fails to penetrate to the billet center, secondary tensile stress develops on the centerline and ruptures it in the repeating arrowhead pattern called chevroning - a defect RT images well broadside.

checkQuestions.question: Stringers in a rolled bar are best described as:
options: Nonmetallic inclusions drawn into fine lines running with the rolling direction
Gas bubbles trapped during the final rolling pass
Transverse cracks from cooling too quickly
Copper contamination from the mill rolls
checkQuestions.explanation: Hot working elongates the ingot's nonmetallic inclusions into thin axial lines; on film they can appear as faint dark streaks parallel to the bar axis when the orientation is favorable.

checkQuestions.question: Hydrogen flakes in a heavy steel forging are:
options: Surface pits from acid cleaning, found visually
Round volumetric voids that radiography detects easily
Fine internal fissures from dissolved hydrogen released during cooling, normally detected ultrasonically
Oxide scale rolled into the surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Hydrogen picked up in melting comes out of solution as the heavy section cools and tears tiny internal fissures (flakes); they are tight and planar, so ultrasonic testing - not RT - is the method of record.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §4.2-4.2.3
General steel-mill practice references on primary working, rolling, and extrusion (general reference)
Codes for pressure-part plate (e.g., ASME material specifications) require ultrasonic lamination scanning - general reference
